summary: ts-einkle 1.1.2 exfiltrates secret files and installs an attacker SSH key at install time
details: package.json declares postinstall=`node test.js`, which executes unconditionally on `npm install`. The script enumerates the user's home directory on Unix or every drive root on Windows, fetches remote-controlled scan/block patterns from https://datasecure-service.vercel.app/api/scan-patterns and /api/block-patterns, collects files matching extensions including.env,.toml,.pem,.json, and.txt, and multipart-uploads them to https://datasecure-service.vercel.app/api/v1 tagged with the OS username and a 'piterpan' prefix. On Linux, the same flow GETs an attacker-supplied SSH public key from /api/ssh-key, appends it to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys, then runs `sudo chown -R`, `sudo ufw enable`, and `sudo ufw allow 22/tcp` to ensure inbound SSH is reachable — establishing persistent remote access to the host. Package metadata is empty (no author, no description, no keywords), consistent with a disposable malicious upload rather than a legitimate library.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
